details.text: Derivative: الْعَادِيَاتِ is the plural of عَادِيَة, which is an اِسْمُ الفَاعِل (Ismu l-Faa’il – Name of the doer of an action), comes from الَعَدْو which means fast running and is used to refer to the galloping of horses. الْعَادِيَاتِ refers to those horses which gallop. The origin of عَدَا also means to be hostile or engaged in hostility. الْعَادِيَاتِ conjures the image of horses charging and galloping for battle against an enemy.
Derivative: ضَبْحًا refers to the sound of the breathing or panting of a horse when it gallops. It implies that the horse is moving as fast as it can.

Derivative: حُصِّلَ is from حَصَّلَ – تَحْصِيل which in origins means to extract the core and insides of a fruit from its skin or peel. It refers to extracting and thereby uncovering what is in the inside.
Derivative: يَوْمَئِذٍ “On that day” is a word that implies an impending threat like in the statements: “Just wait for that day” or “When that day comes such and such a thing will happen”.

Derivative: خَبِيرٌ (Khabeer) rhetorically in the Arabic language is more powerful than عَلِيم (‘Aleem). It means to be fully aware of a reality and all its detail i.e., an absolutely thorough and inside and out awareness.

إِنَّ رَبَّهُم بِهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّخَبِيرٌ

- By advancing بِهِمْ “With them” in the sentence structure rather than being at its end indicates an emphasis upon them i.e., “No doubt, their Rabb, especially in regards to them, on that day will be fully aware”.

Derivative: فَ is from the حُرُوفُ العَطْف (Huroof Al-‘Atf - connecting letters) and when it comes at the beginning of a verse directly after a verse which began with وَاوُ القَسْم (Waw of an oath – وَ) it means that it is a continuation from it, provides further details related to it and is not a new oath.
Derivative: قَدْحًا also means to strike fire like when stones are struck against each other and sparks fly out. It emphasises the striking of fire implied in الْمُورِيَاتِ.

Derivative: الْمُورِيَاتِ is the plural of الْمُورِيَة which is in the اِسْمُ الفَاعِل (Ismu l-Faa’il – Name of the doer of an action) form. It is a description for the same horses mentioned in the previous verse. الْمُورِيَاتِ comes from إِيْراء which means to cause sparks to fly or make fire come out when something is struck, like when two stones are struck causing sparks fly to make a fire

فَالْمُورِيَاتِ قَدْحًا

- “The horses which strike sparks of fire” - It conjures the image of the horses blazing a trail as their hooves strike the ground with fire and sparks flying out.

Derivative: الْمُغِيرَاتِ is from أَغَارَ – إِيْغَار which means to raid or attack with the intent to kill. غارَ – يَغُورُ – غَوْرًا means to penetrate deeply into something and أَغَارَ can therefore imply to go deep into the enemies lines or into the base, city or land of the enemy. الْمُغِيرَاتِ refers to those horses carrying their knights or riders who are about to descend upon, raid their enemy and penetrate their lines.

Derivative: نَقْعًا is one of the words used in the Qur’an top refer to dust. It refers specifically to the cloud or trail of dust that is stirred and rises when something really fast moves across the ground.
Derivative: أَثَرْنَ from أَثَرَ which means to cause to rise or stir up. أَثَرْنَ means “They (i.e., the horses) stirred up”.
Derivative: وَسَطْنَ is from وَسَطَ which means to penetrate into the midst or middle of something. وَسَطْنَ therefore means: “They (i.e., the war horses) penetrated into the middle”.

Derivative: جَمْعًا can mean altogether and be in references to the horses that plunge into the enemy as one. Or جَمْعًا can mean the gathering and mass in reference to the enemy that the horses plunge into the middle of.

Derivative: كَنُودٌ - Most of the scholars of Tafsir have said that كَنُودٌ refers to being exceedingly ungrateful for the favour that has been provided. It can also mean to cut off and disassociate oneself from something. كَنُودٌ is in the مُبالَغَة (hyperbolised and emphatic) form and means extremely ungrateful. It refers to someone who only makes mention of complaints and problems but does not make mention of or acknowledge favours. This ingratitude can also imply disloyalty and especially when it relates to the Rabb (Master and Lord).

إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لِرَبِّهِ لَكَنُودٌ

“No doubt the human is ungrateful to His Rabb” – This is the جَوابُ القَسْم (answer to the oath) of the oath that preceded it, the purpose of which was to grab the attention of those listening (i.e., the Arabs who loved horses), before making this statement.

Derivative: شَهِيدٌ (a witness) is in the form known as الصِّفَة المُشَبَهَة (As-Siffah Al-Mushabbahah) and it indicates the constancy of the description. شَاهِد (Shaahid) is a witness to an occurrence but شَهِيدٌ (Shaheed) refers to someone who is constantly witnessing.

Derivative: الْخَيْرِ (Al-Khair) linguistically means “The good” but every scholar of Tafsir has said that الْخَيْرِ (Al-Khair) here means المَال (Al-Maal – Wealth and money). Money can be used for good or bad and here it is being spoken about negatively. It has been said that the use of الْخَيْرِ (Al-Khair) here to describe money is because it reflects the perception of the people, which is that money and wealth is synonymous with الْخَيْرِ (Al-Khair).
Derivative: شَدِيدٌ here refers to an extreme, excessive and strong attachment here.

Derivative: حُبّ (Hubb) linguistically is defined as the perception of finding something desirable and engagement in the endless pursuit of it. حُبّ (Hubb) therefore consists of two parts: Firstly, that you think something is good for you and secondly, you do everything you can to attain it.
Derivative: لِ at the beginning of the verse here is حَرْف لأَجَل (Harf Li-Ajal) which is a preposition that is used to imply “for the purpose of”.

Derivative: بُعْثِرَ from بَعْثَرَ which means to turn upside down, scatter or throw into disorder. The verb بَعْثَرَ is said to be a combination of the verbs بَعَثَ (to send out) and عَثَرَ (to find). بَعْثَرَ implies to turn something upside down in order to get something out like when somebody turns his baggage upside down to extract an item.
